name: DoorDash, Inc.

profile.description: DoorDash, Inc. operates a logistics platform connecting consumers with local and national businesses in the United States and internationally. The company operates DoorDash Marketplace (food delivery, grocery, convenience, alcohol, flowers, pet supplies), Wolt (international marketplace), and recently acquired Deliveroo (UK/international). Also offers DashPass membership, a Commerce Platform for merchants, and autonomous delivery via partnerships with Waymo and Serve Robotics. Founded in 2013 and headquartered in San Francisco, California.

innovation.label: STRONG
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): DoorDash executed two transformative acquisitions — Wolt in 2022 (\.1B, expanding into 30+ international markets) and Deliveroo in 2025 (\.9B, adding the UK and key European markets) — fundamentally transforming from a US food delivery company into the world's largest local commerce platform [1]. The expansion from restaurant delivery into grocery (#1 third-party grocery marketplace in the US), convenience, alcohol, flowers, and pet supplies represents significant business model evolution. However, the core platform model (connecting consumers, merchants, and Dashers) remains fundamentally unchanged. AI Deployment (2/2): DoorDash has deployed AI across its entire operations stack with measurable results. The proprietary AI-driven dispatch system integrates real-time traffic, Dasher availability, weather, and order volume data to optimize 2B+ deliveries annually [2]. The ChatGPT-powered "Meal to Cart" feature launched in early 2026 enables AI-driven meal planning with one-click grocery delivery. DoorDash Tasks leverages 2M+ US couriers to record audio and video data used to train humanoid robotics and AI models, creating a unique AI data flywheel [2][3]. Market Share (2/2): DoorDash commands approximately 67% of US food delivery market share, making it the dominant #1 player. With the Deliveroo acquisition, the company now operates in 30+ countries. It is also the #1 third-party marketplace for US grocery volume [1][3]. Product Pipeline (0/2): While DoorDash is investing "hundreds of millions" in automation and robotics in 2026, specific next-gen product launch dates are not publicly committed [3]. The Serve Robotics autonomous delivery partnership and DoorDash Dot drone program are in early stages without specific revenue timelines. R&D Intensity (1/1): DoorDash generated \.8B in free cash flow in 2025 and plans to invest hundreds of millions more in 2026, particularly in automation and robotics, representing above-industry R&D commitment for a logistics platform [3].

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): DoorDash's stated mission is to "empower local economies" and "grow and empower local economies everywhere." This is clear and identifies the stakeholders (local businesses and their communities), but it is not deeply distinctive — many logistics and marketplace companies could claim a similar purpose. It connects DoorDash's work to a broader social benefit but doesn't articulate a transformative vision of a future state that inspires sacrifice [1]. Community Impact (1/2): DoorDash launched Project DASH in partnership with the United Way and Feeding America to connect food banks and community organizations with delivery logistics, delivering millions of meals. The company also offers reduced-fee programs for local restaurants. However, the scale relative to DoorDash's B market cap is modest and not a distinctive, large-scale philanthropic investment [4]. Employee Retention (1/2): DoorDash's approximately 19,600 corporate employees operate in a competitive tech labor market. CEO Tony Xu has maintained consistent leadership since founding in 2013, providing stability. The company is integrating Wolt and Deliveroo workforces onto a unified technology platform. However, no specific tenure or turnover data is publicly available, and the 2M+ Dashers are independent contractors with inherently high turnover [4]. Insider Buying (1/2): CEO Tony Xu maintains significant equity ownership and his compensation is heavily equity-based, aligning incentives. However, insiders are net sellers over the past six months, with no notable insider buying activity identified in the last 12 months [3].

infrastructure.label: STRONG
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics for a Growth stock. DoorDash demonstrates strong growth metrics with some profitability constraints. D/E of 32% is well below the 70% target, reflecting conservative leverage even after the \.75B convertible note issuance in Q1 2026 [1][4]. ROE of 9.92% is below the 15% target (score 0.661), reflecting the company's recent transition to profitability. Revenue growth of 28% YoY in FY2025 and 33% in Q1 2026 far exceeds the 10% target [1]. 5-year revenue CAGR of approximately 36.5% (from \.89B in 2020 to .7B in 2025) is exceptional. Forward P/E of 32.63 scores 0.684, reflecting premium valuation for a high-growth platform. Profit margin of 6.29% is the weakest metric (score 0.315), as DoorDash is still in early-stage profitability with FY2025 being only its second year of GAAP net income [1]. Forward PEG of 0.79 is attractive, below the 1.0 target, earning maximum score on 2x weight. Liquidity floor easily cleared at <M+ daily dollar volume [4].

ai_resilience.label: MODERATE
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (4/9): DoorDash faces meaningful AI disruption risk from two vectors. First, consumer AI agents could bypass delivery platform apps entirely by autonomously shopping across multiple platforms, finding the lowest fees and fastest delivery — effectively disintermediating DoorDash's consumer-facing marketplace. Citrini Research has specifically warned that "consumer AI agents could bypass delivery apps altogether," threatening DoorDash's take rate and customer relationship [5]. Second, autonomous delivery (self-driving vehicles, robots, drones) could reduce barriers to entry by eliminating the need for a massive Dasher network, which is DoorDash's primary competitive moat. However, DoorDash's physical logistics network, merchant relationships across 30+ countries, 2M+ Dashers, and the "last 50 feet" problem provide meaningful near-term protection. The business involves real-world physical delivery that AI alone cannot replace — food still needs to physically move from restaurant to doorstep. The company scores in the EXPOSED range because while the core physical delivery is protected, the platform/marketplace layer faces genuine AI substitution risk. AI Opportunity (1/1): DoorDash generates meaningful AI-driven revenue through its AI dispatch optimization (processing 2B+ deliveries annually), the ChatGPT-powered Meal to Cart feature, and DoorDash Tasks which leverages couriers for AI training data collection. The company is also monetizing AI through its merchant Commerce Platform with AI-powered marketing and analytics tools [2][3].

infinite_game.summary: DoorDash under founder-CEO Tony Xu shows some infinite game characteristics — consistent founder leadership since 2013, willingness to invest in long-term capabilities (autonomous delivery, international expansion), and evolution from food delivery to a broader local commerce platform. However, the company's competitive language frequently emphasizes market share dominance and "winning," the gig worker model creates structural trust challenges, and the Just Cause is not deeply articulated beyond growing local economies. Sinek would see a company with some infinite instincts but a fundamentally finite competitive framework.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (1/3): DoorDash's mission to "empower local economies" exists but reads more as a marketing narrative than a deeply held conviction that shapes strategic decisions. The company's actual strategic behavior — aggressive market share competition with Uber Eats, massive acquisitions of Wolt and Deliveroo, expansion into every delivery vertical — is driven more by growth and scale logic than by a vision of empowered local economies [1]. The mission doesn't clearly describe a future state that does not yet exist. Sinek would note that "empowering local economies" is interchangeable with many logistics and marketplace companies and doesn't pass the resilience test — it hasn't been tested through crisis or trade-off moments where the company chose local economy empowerment over growth.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): DoorDash's approximately 19,600 corporate employees operate under consistent founder leadership from Tony Xu since 2013. The integration of Wolt and Deliveroo teams onto a unified global technology platform suggests organizational maturity. However, the company's core labor model relies on 2M+ independent contractor Dashers who have no job security, benefits, or organizational belonging — this is structurally antithetical to Sinek's Circle of Safety [4]. Multiple legal and regulatory challenges over gig worker classification (Proposition 22 in California, EU gig worker directives) highlight the tension between DoorDash's business model and worker trust. For the corporate organization specifically, no red flags were identified, but no exceptional trust indicators either.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): DoorDash acknowledges Uber Eats and Instacart as competitive forces and has used competitive pressure to continuously improve its platform — the expansion into grocery was partly motivated by Instacart's strength, and international expansion via Wolt and Deliveroo responded to Uber's global presence. Management commentary focuses on improving value for customers and merchants rather than destroying competitors, though the company's dominant 67% US food delivery share does create some market-power dynamics [1][3].

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): DoorDash has meaningfully evolved from a single-category food delivery startup into a multi-category local commerce platform operating in 30+ countries. The Wolt acquisition (\.1B) and Deliveroo acquisition (\.9B) represent significant strategic bets on international scale. The expansion into grocery, convenience, alcohol, and the launch of DoorDash Tasks (leveraging couriers for AI data collection) show adaptive thinking [1][3]. However, these are adjacent expansions of the core delivery platform model, not transformative reinventions. The company has not abandoned a profitable business in service of a larger purpose — it has scaled the same model into new categories and geographies.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Tony Xu demonstrated courage by investing "hundreds of millions" in autonomous delivery and robotics before these technologies generate meaningful revenue, and by executing the \.9B Deliveroo acquisition to build international scale even as profitability was just emerging [1][3]. The launch of DoorDash Tasks, turning 2M couriers into an AI training data workforce, represents innovative long-term thinking. However, these investments are funded by \.8B in annual free cash flow, reducing the relative risk. Sinek would note that truly courageous decisions involve painful trade-offs — DoorDash's aggressive growth spending is more characteristic of a growth-stage tech company than a company making costly principled choices.

investment_thesis: DoorDash delivered Q1 2026 revenue of \.0B (up 33% YoY) with the Deliveroo acquisition accelerating international scale, and FY2025 revenue of .7B (up 28%) with net income of M — only the company's second full year of GAAP profitability [1]. The platform's competitive moat is formidable: 67% US food delivery market share, #1 third-party grocery marketplace, 2M+ active Dashers, and operations across 30+ countries post-Deliveroo. At a forward PEG of 0.79 with revenue growing 33% and projected 5-year CAGR of 27%+, the growth-adjusted valuation is attractive. The \.8B in 2025 free cash flow funds aggressive investment in autonomous delivery, AI-powered features like Meal to Cart, and the unified global technology platform integrating DoorDash, Wolt, and Deliveroo [3].
thesis_continued: The primary concern is the gap between DoorDash's growth story and its profitability reality. Net profit margin of 6.3% is thin for an B market cap company, and the trailing P/E of 87x prices in years of margin expansion that may not materialize given competitive pressure from Uber Eats and structural headwinds from gig worker regulation [1][4]. AI agents represent an existential medium-term threat — consumer AI that autonomously shops across delivery platforms could eliminate DoorDash's customer relationship and compress take rates, as Citrini Research has warned [5]. The \.75B in 0% convertible notes issued in Q1 2026 add dilution risk at conversion. International integration risk is high — Deliveroo's UK business operates in a more regulated, lower-margin market, and combining three technology platforms (DoorDash, Wolt, Deliveroo) creates significant execution complexity. Autonomous delivery is years from meaningful scale and requires massive capital investment that could pressure margins before generating returns.
key_catalysts: Deliveroo acquisition integration driving international revenue scale, with the combined entity projected to contribute approximately M in incremental adjusted EBITDA as DoorDash, Wolt, and Deliveroo unify onto a single global technology platform

ChatGPT-powered Meal to Cart feature and AI-driven dispatch optimization enhancing user engagement and operational efficiency, with record Q1 2026 membership signups and monthly active user highs demonstrating platform stickiness
Forward PEG of 0.79 at 33% revenue growth represents attractive risk-reward if DoorDash successfully expands margins toward 10%+ net margin through operating leverage and automation investment
key_risks: Consumer AI agents could disintermediate delivery platform apps entirely by autonomously shopping across competitors for lowest fees and fastest delivery, threatening DoorDash's 67% US market share and customer relationship — Citrini Research has specifically flagged this agentic commerce risk

Net profit margin of 6.3% is structurally thin for an B market cap company, with gig worker regulation (EU directives, Proposition 22 challenges) threatening to increase labor costs and compress the already narrow margin profile

Integration of three technology platforms (DoorDash, Wolt, Deliveroo) across 30+ countries creates significant execution complexity, with the \.75B convertible note issuance adding dilution risk if the stock appreciates above conversion price
